comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses material recognition by selecting and integrating deep features from multiple CNNs trained on different tasks (object vs. material datasets). The main technical contribution is a filter-based feature selection method that treats each individual activation as a candidate “weak classifier”: images maximizing a feature are collected, class-entropy over that set is computed, and a boosting-like iterative scheme selects features minimizing weighted entropy while penalizing covered samples. The authors also introduce EFMD, an expanded version of FMD (10x larger) collected via AMT. Experiments on FMD, EFMD and MINC show state-of-the-art or competitive performance (notably 84.0% ± 1.8% on FMD, close to reported human performance). The paper analyzes robustness to parameters and studies classifier diversity to explain when selection helps.

Strengths:
- Well-motivated: leveraging object cues for material recognition is plausible and supported by prior literature.
- Simple, computationally efficient selection criterion using activation-maximizing samples and entropy; the boosting-like weighting is intuitive.
- Empirical validation across multiple datasets including a newly constructed EFMD; achieves strong accuracy on FMD and competitive results on MINC.
- Reasonable analysis of parameter sensitivity and investigation of when the method helps (diversity/entropy analyses).

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Originality is moderate: the method composes several existing ideas (activation-based visualization, entropy-based ranking, boosting-style weighting) rather than proposing a fundamentally new learning paradigm.
- Methodological details required for reproducibility are insufficient in places: exact fine-tuning hyperparameters (learning rates, number of iterations/epochs), SVM hyperparameter tuning, preprocessing/cropping protocols, and code availability are not specified in the provided text.
- Comparison to prior work is somewhat limited. The paper cites related approaches (e.g., FV-CNN, multi-scale approaches) but direct apples-to-apples comparisons are difficult because training data and protocols differ; stronger controlled baselines and ablations would clarify the benefit of the selection method itself (e.g., end-to-end fine-tuning on concatenated features, random feature selection baselines, or comparisons with other feature selection methods).
- The choice of using only fc7 activations (no multi-scale or conv-layer pooled descriptors) may miss spatial or multi-scale cues known to help texture/material tasks; this is noted but not explored.
- The theoretical justification of the entropy-based objective and the weighting update (Eq. 4) is heuristic; more discussion of its properties or guarantees would strengthen the contribution.
- While EFMD construction is described, more information on dataset curation and statistics (examples per class, inter-annotator agreement, availability) would be useful.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide full implementation details and release code and EFMD to facilitate reproducibility.
- Include more ablation studies: (i) effect of using conv-layer Fisher Vector/pooled features vs. fc7, (ii) comparison to other selection methods (mutual information, mRMR, wrapper-based selection), (iii) end-to-end baselines where concatenated features are fine-tuned jointly.
- Add qualitative visualizations of selected features and their top images to illustrate what types of cues are chosen (object parts, textures, colors).
- Clarify SVM training protocol and hyperparameter selection; report runtime and memory costs of the selection algorithm.
- Discuss limitations more explicitly (when selection fails, sensitivity to dataset bias, risk of overfitting when EFMD is similar to FMD).

Conclusion:
The paper presents a sensible, practical approach to integrate heterogeneous deep features for material recognition and supplies a useful new dataset. Empirical results are strong, and the analysis is informative. However, the novelty is incremental and some experimental and reproducibility details are missing. After addressing the above concerns (especially more controlled baselines and release of code/data), this work would be a valuable contribution to the material recognition literature.
